title: US: Civil Construction Induction
subtitle: Entry-Level Safety and Compliance — United States
description: Essential OSHA knowledge for civil construction workers in the United States. Covers 29 CFR 1926, competent person requirements, excavation safety, crane operations, and MUTCD traffic control standards.

lessons.title: The OSH Act and OSHA Overview
lessons.content:
**The Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970 (OSH Act)**
The OSH Act created OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration) and established the federal right to a safe workplace.

**Key Provision — General Duty Clause (Section 5(a)(1)):**
Every employer must provide a workplace free from recognised hazards that are causing or likely to cause death or serious physical harm. This applies even when no specific OSHA standard exists.

**Worker Rights Under OSHA:**
- Right to a safe and healthful workplace
- Right to know about workplace hazards (Hazard Communication — HazCom)
- Right to request an OSHA inspection
- Right to be protected from retaliation for reporting hazards
- Right to see OSHA 300 injury log

**Key Construction Standard:**
29 CFR Part 1926 — Safety and Health Regulations for Construction
This is the primary OSHA standard for all civil and building construction work in the USA.

**State Plans:**
26 states run their own OSHA-approved State Plans (e.g., Cal/OSHA in California). State plans must be at least as effective as federal OSHA. The other 24 states use federal OSHA directly.

lessons.title: OSHA 10 and OSHA 30 Training
lessons.content:
**OSHA Outreach Training — The US Industry Standard**

While not always legally required, OSHA 10 and OSHA 30 training is the widely recognised standard for construction workers in the USA.

**OSHA 10-Hour (Construction):**
- 10 hours of safety training covering OSHA standards
- Designed for entry-level workers
- Covers: Fall protection, electrical, struck-by, caught-in hazards (the Fatal Four)
- Card issued: OSHA 10 card (sometimes called DOL card)
- No expiry — but some states/projects require renewal

**OSHA 30-Hour (Construction):**
- 30 hours — designed for supervisors and safety personnel
- Deeper coverage of OSHA standards, rights and responsibilities

**The Fatal Four — Leading Causes of Construction Deaths (OSHA):**
1. Falls (39% of construction deaths)
2. Struck-by objects (8%)
3. Electrocution (8%)
4. Caught-in/between (1%)

**OSHA Recordkeeping — 300 Log:**
Employers with 10+ workers must maintain OSHA Form 300 (injury log), 300A (summary), and 301 (incident report).

questions.q: The General Duty Clause requires employers to:
options: Provide a workplace free from recognised hazards
Report all injuries to OSHA within 24 hours

questions.q: The primary OSHA construction standard is:
Which is the number one cause of construction deaths in the USA?
OSHA 10-Hour training is designed for:

lessons.title: Excavation and Trenching — 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P
lessons.content:
**Excavation Safety — One of the Most Critical Topics in US Civil Construction**

OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P covers all excavation and trenching work. Trenching is one of the most hazardous construction operations in the USA.

**Key Definitions:**
- Excavation: Any man-made cut, cavity, trench, or depression in the ground
- Trench: A narrow excavation (in relation to length) made below the surface — generally less than 15 feet wide

**The Competent Person Requirement:**
A competent person must:
- Classify the soil before excavation begins
- Inspect excavations daily and after any event that could affect safety
- Have authority to stop work immediately if a hazard exists

**Soil Classification (OSHA):**
- Type A: Most stable (hard clay, cemented soils) — can use 3/4:1 slope
- Type B: Intermediate — 1:1 slope required
- Type C: Least stable (granular soils, wet conditions) — 1.5:1 slope required

**Protective Systems (any excavation 5 feet or deeper):**
1. Sloping — cut walls back to safe angle
2. Shoring — supports trench walls (timber, hydraulic, or pneumatic)
3. Trench box/shield — steel cage that protects workers

**Egress Requirement:**
A ladder or ramp must be provided in trenches 4 feet deep or more, within 25 feet of every worker.

lessons.title: Competent Person — The US Safety Linchpin
lessons.content:
**What is a Competent Person?**
In US construction, the Competent Person is one of the most important concepts. A Competent Person is someone who:

1. Is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards
2. Has authority to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate hazards

**Required By OSHA In:**
- Excavations (Subpart P)
- Scaffolding (Subpart L)
- Fall Protection (Subpart M)
- Cranes and Derricks (Subpart CC)
- Concrete and Masonry (Subpart Q)
- Steel Erection (Subpart R)

**Competent Person vs Qualified Person:**
- Competent Person: Experience and authority to identify and fix hazards
- Qualified Person: Has specific training, education, or credentials (e.g., engineer, certified rigger)

**No Federal Excavator Licence:**
Like Australia, the USA has no federal government licence for excavator operation. The employer must ensure the operator is trained and competent for the specific equipment.

**811 — Call Before You Dig:**
US national one-call number. Call 811 at least 2 business days before any excavation. Free service — utility locators mark underground lines.

questions.q: A Competent Person must have authority to:
options: Stop work immediately if a hazard exists

questions.q: A trench 5 feet or deeper requires:
options: No protection if workers are careful
A protective system (sloping, shoring, or trench box)
Only a competent person on site

lessons.title: Fall Protection — OSHA Subpart M
lessons.content:
**Fall Protection in US Construction — 29 CFR 1926 Subpart M**

**Fall Protection Required at 6 Feet**
US construction: fall protection required at 6 feet above a lower level.
(Compare: NZ and Australia — 2 metres; Canada — 3 metres)

**Three Primary Fall Protection Systems:**
1. Guardrail systems — top rail at 42 inches (+/- 3 inches)
2. Safety net systems — installed within 30 feet below working level
3. Personal fall arrest systems (PFAS) — harness + lanyard + anchor

**Personal Fall Arrest System Requirements:**
- Full body harness (body belts no longer allowed for arrest)
- Shock-absorbing lanyard or self-retracting lifeline
- Anchor point: 5,000 lbs capacity minimum
- Free fall limited to 6 feet
- Total fall arrest distance cannot exceed 18.5 feet

**Hole Covers:**
All floor/roof holes must be covered or protected. Cover must:
- Support twice the load of workers and equipment
- Be marked HOLE or COVER
- Be secured against movement

lessons.title: HazCom and PPE Requirements
lessons.content:
**Hazard Communication Standard (HazCom) — 29 CFR 1926.59**

The US Hazard Communication Standard (HazCom) aligns with the Globally Harmonized System (GHS) — similar to Canada's WHMIS 2015.

**HazCom Three Elements:**
1. Safety Data Sheets (SDS) — required for all hazardous chemicals
2. Labels — GHS-format on all containers
3. Training — workers must understand SDS and labels

**Required PPE on US Construction Sites:**
- Hard hat (ANSI/ISEA Z89.1 — Type I or II, Class E for electrical)
- High-visibility vest (ANSI 107 — Class 2 minimum for roadwork)
- Safety footwear (ASTM F2413)
- Eye and face protection (ANSI Z87.1)
- Hearing protection where noise exceeds 90 dB (8-hour TWA)

**Silica Rule — 29 CFR 1926.1153:**
OSHA's Respirable Crystalline Silica Standard for Construction requires:
- Action Level: 25 micrograms per cubic meter (8-hr TWA)
- Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL): 50 micrograms per cubic meter
- Engineering controls first (wet methods, vacuum systems)
- Respirator: N95 or better when controls insufficient

questions.q: In US construction, fall protection is required at:
Full body harnesses replaced body belts for fall arrest because:
options: Body belts can cause internal injuries during a fall
They are easier to put on

lessons.title: MUTCD — Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices
lessons.content:
**MUTCD — The National Traffic Control Standard**
The Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) MUTCD sets national standards for all traffic control devices on roads open to public travel.

**Part 6 — Temporary Traffic Control (TTC):**
Covers all traffic management for roadwork and construction zones.

**Key TTC Components:**
- Advance warning signs — alert drivers before the work zone
- Tapers — merge lanes using cones and channelizing devices
- Activity area — where the actual work occurs
- Termination area — transition back to normal traffic

**Flagger Requirements (MUTCD Part 6E):**
- Must be trained and experienced
- Wear ANSI 107 Class 2 or 3 high-visibility apparel
- Use STOP/SLOW paddle (preferred over flags)
- Positioned for maximum visibility

**State DOTs:**
- Each state has its own Department of Transportation
- California: Caltrans | Texas: TxDOT | New York: NYSDOT | Florida: FDOT
- States may adopt the MUTCD with supplements

**Work Zone Speed Fines:**
Most states double fines for traffic violations in active work zones.

lessons.title: AASHTO and Technical Standards
lessons.content:
**AASHTO — American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials**

AASHTO is the US equivalent of Austroads (Australia/NZ) and TAC (Canada). It develops technical standards for road design and materials.

**Key AASHTO Standards:**
- AASHTO Guide for Design of Pavement Structures
- AASHTO M and R standards (materials and test methods)
- AASHTO T99/T180 — Standard Proctor and Modified Proctor compaction tests

**ASTM International — Key Standards:**
- ASTM D698 — Standard Proctor compaction test
- ASTM D1557 — Modified Proctor compaction test
- ASTM D6938 — Nuclear density gauge testing
- ASTM C136 — Sieve analysis (aggregate gradation)

**Crane Operations — OSHA Subpart CC:**
- Crane operators must be certified (NCCCO — National Commission for the Certification of Crane Operators)
- OR qualified by an audited employer program
- Crane assembly/disassembly requires a Qualified Person
- Annual crane inspections required by qualified person

**No Federal Excavator Licence:**
The USA has no federal licence for excavators. A Competent Person must verify operator training and competency.

questions.q: The MUTCD is published by:
AASHTO is best described as:
options: The US equivalent of Austroads — road design and materials standards

questions.q: Crane operators in the USA must be:
options: NCCCO certified or qualified by an audited employer program

questions.q: ASTM D698 covers:

lessons.title: Course Summary
lessons.content:
**Congratulations — You have completed US: Civil Construction Induction!**

Key knowledge covered:

- OSH Act 1970: General Duty Clause, worker rights, OSHA 10/30
- The Fatal Four: Falls, Struck-by, Electrocution, Caught-in
- 29 CFR 1926: The primary US construction safety standard
- Competent Person: Must identify hazards AND have authority to fix them
- Excavations: 5ft = protective system required, 4ft = ladder required
- Soil types: Type A (stable) to Type C (unstable)
- Fall protection at 6 feet (not 2m like NZ/AU)
- MUTCD Part 6: Traffic control standard for work zones
- AASHTO and ASTM: Technical standards for roads and materials
- 811: Call before you dig (national)

**Key Differences vs NZ:**
- US fall protection trigger: 6 feet (NZ: 2 metres)
- 26 states have their own OSHA plans
- Competent Person concept is central to US compliance
- No federal excavator licence in USA

questions.q: The General Duty Clause requires:
options: Provide workplace free from recognised hazards
Hire a safety officer for every 10 workers
